critic_raw:
Three issues undermine this design.

First, all preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders) are fit on the complete dataset before splitting. The design explicitly states this. For an EHR dataset spanning 5 years, this introduces leakage from future data into normalization, encoding, and vocabulary steps applied during model training.

Second, in the validation section, the design states: 'After hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, a final check is performed using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed.' Using the test set to 'fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed' is a direct violation of the held-out test set principle. Once the test set is consulted to inform architecture decisions, it is no longer an unbiased evaluator of generalization performance.

Third, the split uses stratified random sampling across the full 5-year EHR dataset without enforcing patient-level hold-out. The design states 'patient-level stratification to prevent patient leakage' but does not enforce that all encounters from a single patient appear in one split. If 100,000+ visits include repeat patients (highly likely in a 5-year hospital dataset), patient-level leakage will inflate test performance.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on full dataset — leakage into training pipeline
Test set used for architecture fine-tuning after hyperparameter tuning — compromises held-out evaluation
Patient-level split not enforced despite claiming 'patient-level stratification' to prevent leakage

round_transcripts.critic:
Three issues undermine this design.

First, all preprocessing transformers (scalers, categorical encoders, vocabulary builders) are fit on the complete dataset before splitting. The design explicitly states this. For an EHR dataset spanning 5 years, this introduces leakage from future data into normalization, encoding, and vocabulary steps applied during model training.

Second, in the validation section, the design states: 'After hyperparameter tuning on the validation set, a final check is performed using the test set to ensure robustness of model selection and to fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed.' Using the test set to 'fine-tune model architecture if discrepancies are observed' is a direct violation of the held-out test set principle. Once the test set is consulted to inform architecture decisions, it is no longer an unbiased evaluator of generalization performance.

Third, the split uses stratified random sampling across the full 5-year EHR dataset without enforcing patient-level hold-out. The design states 'patient-level stratification to prevent patient leakage' but does not enforce that all encounters from a single patient appear in one split. If 100,000+ visits include repeat patients (highly likely in a 5-year hospital dataset), patient-level leakage will inflate test performance.
round_transcripts.defender:
On preprocessing leakage: for vocabulary builders and clinical code encoders over 5 years of EHR data, fitting on the full dataset ensures the vocabulary includes all diagnostic codes encountered in validation and test periods. A training-only vocabulary would create out-of-vocabulary tokens for new diagnostic codes introduced in later years, degrading model performance for reasons unrelated to the hypothesis. The leakage from vocabulary fitting is informational but not outcome-predictive.

On test set use: the design language about 'fine-tuning model architecture if discrepancies are observed' after consulting the test set is problematic. The defense cannot justify this practice. If test set results are used to inform architecture changes, the test set is contaminated. The design should state that the test set is evaluated exactly once with the final model.

On patient-level leakage: the design's statement about 'patient-level stratification' appears to describe stratification by patient characteristics (age, SES) for class balance purposes, not enforcement that each patient's encounters are in one split. The critic correctly identifies this ambiguity. For a 5-year dataset, repeat hospitalizations within a patient will create leakage if not explicitly grouped.

round_transcripts.critic: Issue 2 (test set contamination) is explicitly conceded by the defense — the design language is described as 'problematic' and 'cannot be justified.' Issue 3 (patient-level grouping) is acknowledged as an ambiguity that creates leakage if repeat hospitalizations occur. Issue 1 is partially defended but vocabulary fitting on full data still constitutes leakage. Two of three issues are acknowledged as genuine flaws.
round_transcripts.defender: Issue 2 is a genuine flaw. Issue 3 is a genuine ambiguity with leakage implications. Issue 1 is defended on practical grounds but methodologically imperfect.

all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing vocabulary/encoders fit on full dataset — partial defense, still methodologically imperfect
Test set used for architecture fine-tuning — explicitly conceded as genuine flaw
Patient-level grouping not enforced — leakage acknowledged
